summary: Gruber argues that Google CEO Eric Schmidt is fundamentally creepy, citing a pattern of unsettling statements about privacy, personal data, and Google's role in people's lives. Schmidt's WSJ interview remarks—that Google should tell people what to do next and that young people will need to change their names to escape their digital past—exemplify this creepiness. Gruber theorizes that the tension between Google's original 'Don't be evil' ethos and its increasingly invasive behavior maps onto a cultural rift between Larry/Sergey and Schmidt. The founders represent the cool, innovative Google, while Schmidt represents the ad-driven, privacy-indifferent Google. Google's controversial alliance with Verizon on net neutrality fits the Schmidt side perfectly.
key_points: Schmidt's public statements about privacy and Google's role reveal a disturbing comfort with surveillance and paternalism
Schmidt's deflection in Abu Dhabi—'Would you prefer someone else?'—dodges the real question of whether anyone should hold that much personal data
Gruber identifies a cultural rift within Google between the Larry/Sergey engineering ethos and Schmidt's ad-revenue-driven pragmatism
The Google-Verizon net neutrality alliance is consistent with Schmidt's willingness to compromise principles for business advantage
The real question isn't who should hold massive personal data stores, but whether anyone should
money_quotes: Here's my theory: the problem with Google is that Eric Schmidt is creepy. I think he's a really weird dude.
But Schmidt doesn't seem eccentric (or at least not merely so). He seems creepy.
Maybe the question isn't who should hold this information, but rather should anyone hold this information.
That's a glib answer, but at least our government answers to its citizens through elections. Schmidt answers to Google shareholders.
On the other, the Schmidt Google that, in its efforts to serve ads as efficiently as possible, no longer seems concerned with the traditional Western concept of personal privacy.
That stance doesn't fit with my view of the Larry/Sergey Google. But it fits my idea of the Schmidt Google like a glove.
key_insight: Google's growing disregard for privacy isn't a company-wide failing but a reflection of Eric Schmidt's personal comfort with surveillance, representing a cultural schism between Google's engineering idealism and its advertising-driven pragmatism.
